abstract: We introduce a self-propelled colloidal "docker" made of hematite that can be precision-steered to particle cargo many times its own size. Once activated by visible light, the docker can attach to, transport, and subsequently release the cargo at a desired location. This entire sequence of self-propulsion and docking is fully reversible and light-sensitive. Navigation is achieved either through a weak, uniform magnetic field or by utilizing nanoscale tracks etched into a textured substrate. The underlying mechanism for both motion and docking is rooted in osmotic and phoretic transport within a fuel concentration gradient (hydrogen peroxide), which is sustained by the photocatalytic properties of the hematite. Unlike complex multi-component micro-robots, these hematite dockers are simple, single-component particles that can be synthesized in bulk. This versatile system offers a robust platform for developing micrometer-scale factories and sophisticated biomimetic systems.
